topic: Testing - Unit Tests in Same File

turns.content: I'm nervous about the interview because I thought unit tests needed to be a separate binary file like cargo test runs, so I've been trying to write my logic and tests in different crates. But what if I just put everything in one module?

That's actually a common misconception; you don't need separate files for unit tests at all in Rust. You can define the #[test] attribute directly on functions within your main crate, and those test functions get compiled into your binary automatically when you run cargo test. The key mechanism here is using #[cfg(test)] to wrap conditional compilation so that test code only exists in a debug build context.

Ah, I see! So if I add #[test] before my function and wrap it with #[cfg(test)], then cargo test will pick it up without needing extra configuration? Can I still assert values like assert_eq! or use should_panic inside these functions?

Exactly, you can use any of the standard macros like assert_eq!, assert_ne!, and even should_panic directly in your test functions. These macros are available from std::test or the testing crates depending on what you need, but they integrate seamlessly into #[cfg(test)] blocks. You might also want to include println! inside your tests if you're debugging during development since those outputs appear in the console when tests run.
